exercises.title: Build QFT Matrix
exercises.description: Implement the QFT matrix for n qubits and verify it's unitary.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n_qubits):
    """Create QFT matrix for n qubits."""
    N = 2**n_qubits
    # Your code here
    pass

QFT = qft_matrix(2)
print('QFT shape:', QFT.shape)
print('Is unitary:', np.allclose(QFT @ QFT.conj().T, np.eye(4)))
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n_qubits):
    """Create QFT matrix for n qubits."""
    N = 2**n_qubits
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    F = np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)])
    return F / np.sqrt(N)

QFT = qft_matrix(2)
print('QFT matrix:')
print(np.round(QFT, 3))
print('\nIs unitary:', np.allclose(QFT @ QFT.conj().T, np.eye(4)))

exercises.title: QFT on Basis States
exercises.description: Apply QFT to each basis state and observe the resulting superpositions.
exercises.starterCode:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

QFT = qft_matrix(2)

# Apply to each basis state
for j in range(4):
    basis = np.zeros(4)
    basis[j] = 1
    result = QFT @ basis
    print(f'QFT|{j}⟩ = {np.round(result, 3)}')
exercises.solution:
import numpy as np

def qft_matrix(n):
    N = 2**n
    omega = np.exp(2j * np.pi / N)
    return np.array([[omega**(j*k) for k in range(N)] for j in range(N)]) / np.sqrt(N)

QFT = qft_matrix(2)

print('QFT on 2-qubit basis states:')
for j in range(4):
    basis = np.zeros(4)
    basis[j] = 1
    result = QFT @ basis
    print(f'QFT|{j}⟩ = {np.round(result, 3)}')
    # Show phases
    phases = np.angle(result) / np.pi
    print(f'  Phases (×π): {np.round(phases, 2)}')
hints: Higher j gives more complex phases
